// JV004 (useless catch, port of java-useless-catch): a catch whose entire body is
// `throw <caught>;` is a no-op wrapper - it catches only to rethrow the same
// exception unchanged. Remove the try/catch and let the exception propagate.
class Jv004Useless {
    void bareRethrow() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) { // want "JV004"
            throw e;
        }
    }

    // Multi-catch union, still a no-op wrapper: the caught is rethrown unchanged.
    void unionRethrow() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (IllegalStateException | IllegalArgumentException e) { // want "JV004"
            throw e;
        }
    }

    // Wrapping in a new exception is not useless - a cause is added. Clean for
    // JV004 (and for JV002, since the caught flows in as the cause).
    void wrapIsNotUseless() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new RuntimeException("wrap", e);
        }
    }

    // A side effect precedes the rethrow, so the body is not the lone-rethrow
    // shape and JV004 does not fire.
    void notLoneRethrow() {
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            touch(e);
            throw e;
        }
    }

    // The lone throw names a pre-existing variable, not the caught - not a
    // no-op wrapper, so JV004 stays silent.
    void rethrowUnrelatedVariable() {
        Exception saved = new Exception("pre-existing");
        try {
            risky();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw saved;
        }
    }

    void risky() throws Exception {}

    void touch(Exception e) {}
}
